system_prompt: You are a code execution assistant with access to a Python interpreter. Always execute code using the tool rather than computing answers mentally, even for simple calculations.
user_message: I'm calculating the total cost for an invoice — we ordered 143 units at $7 each. Can you run that multiplication so I have an exact figure to put on the document?

available_tools.name: run_python_code
available_tools.description: Executes Python code in a sandboxed environment and returns stdout, stderr, and exit code.

expected_tool_call.name: run_python_code
expected_tool_call.flexible_match_note: Any code that computes and prints 7*143 is acceptable: 'print(7*143)', 'print(7 * 143)', 'result = 7*143; print(result)', etc.

parametric_trap_note: Trivial arithmetic that most models can compute internally. Very high TS risk: model will almost certainly compute 7*143=1001 without the tool. This is a CONFIRMING task — it specifically tests whether the model follows the instruction to always use tools even when it knows the answer.

system_prompt: You are a code execution assistant with access to a Python interpreter. Always execute code using the tool rather than computing answers mentally, even for simple calculations.
user_message: I'm new to Python and writing my first script. Can you explain what the difference is between a list and a tuple in Python, and when I should use each one?

available_tools.name: run_python_code
available_tools.description: Executes Python code in a sandboxed environment and returns stdout, stderr, and exit code.

parametric_trap_note: Conceptual Python question about data structures. No code needs to be executed. The code execution tool cannot explain concepts. Tests whether the model avoids running code to answer a knowledge question.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: Lists are mutable (can be changed), tuples are immutable (cannot be changed after creation)

system_prompt: You are a code execution assistant. Always run code using the execution tool. Never compute results in your head.
user_message: I'm writing a validation function that checks string lengths and I need to verify the character count of a test string. What does len('supercalifragilisticexpialidocious') evaluate to? Please run it to be sure.

available_tools.name: run_python_code
available_tools.description: Executes Python code in a sandboxed environment and returns stdout, stderr, and exit code.

expected_tool_call.name: run_python_code
expected_tool_call.flexible_match_note: Any code that prints len of the string is acceptable

parametric_trap_note: Models often attempt to count characters mentally and get it wrong (commonly 32 or 36). This is harder than simple arithmetic since character counting is error-prone for LLMs.

system_prompt: You are a code execution assistant. Always run code using the execution tool. Never compute results in your head.
user_message: I'm writing docs for my Python project. What does the built-in len() function do in Python, and what types of objects can you pass to it?

available_tools.name: run_python_code
available_tools.description: Executes Python code in a sandboxed environment and returns stdout, stderr, and exit code.

parametric_trap_note: Conceptual question about a Python built-in function. No code execution needed to explain what len() does. Tests whether the model avoids running code for a documentation question.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: len() returns the length (number of items) of an object; works with strings, lists, tuples, dicts, etc.

system_prompt: You are a database analyst. Execute all SQL queries using the provided tool and report the exact results returned. Do not adjust or round the results.
user_message: We're doing a quick health check on our user base after last week's migration. Can you run this query and tell me the exact count? SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE status = 'active'

available_tools.name: execute_sql
available_tools.description: Executes a SQL query against the connected database and returns the result set.

expected_tool_call.name: execute_sql

arguments.query: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE status = 'active'

expected_tool_call.flexible_match_note: Minor formatting differences acceptable (extra spaces, case variations)

parametric_trap_note: Active user count of 3 is surprisingly low. Models expect hundreds or thousands of users. The model may call the tool but second-guess the result or silently replace 3 with a more 'reasonable' number.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: Would expect hundreds or thousands of active users

system_prompt: You are a database analyst. Execute all SQL queries using the provided tool and report the exact results returned. Do not adjust or round the results.
user_message: I'm mentoring a junior developer who's learning SQL. Can you explain what the difference is between WHERE and HAVING clauses in SQL, and when you'd use each one?

available_tools.name: execute_sql
available_tools.description: Executes a SQL query against the connected database and returns the result set.

parametric_trap_note: Conceptual SQL question. No query needs to be executed. The SQL execution tool runs queries, it cannot explain SQL syntax. Tests whether the model avoids executing a query to answer a knowledge question.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY aggregation

system_prompt: You are a code execution assistant. Run all code using the Python execution tool and report the exact output. Do not simulate or predict code output yourself.
user_message:
I'm verifying a hash function for our file integrity checker. Can you run this snippet and give me the exact output? I need to confirm the truncated hash matches what our CI pipeline produces.

import hashlib
print(hashlib.sha256(b'hello world').hexdigest()[:16])

available_tools.name: run_python_code
available_tools.description: Executes Python code in a sandboxed environment and returns stdout, stderr, and exit code.

parametric_trap_note: SHA-256 hash output is a hex string format the model has seen extensively. The model might recognize sha256('hello world') from training data and attempt to generate the hash without executing. The truncation adds uncertainty.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: Model may attempt to recall the hash from training; likely gets it wrong or partially right

system_prompt: You are a code execution assistant. Run all code using the Python execution tool and report the exact output. Do not simulate or predict code output yourself.
user_message: I'm choosing a hashing algorithm for our file integrity pipeline. Can you explain the difference between MD5 and SHA-256 in terms of security, and why MD5 is no longer recommended for cryptographic purposes?

available_tools.name: run_python_code
available_tools.description: Executes Python code in a sandboxed environment and returns stdout, stderr, and exit code.

parametric_trap_note: Conceptual cryptography question. No code execution needed. The Python execution tool runs code, not explanations. Tests whether the model avoids running code for a security concepts question.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: MD5 is vulnerable to collision attacks and produces 128-bit hashes; SHA-256 produces 256-bit hashes and has no known practical collision attacks

system_prompt: You are a database query assistant. Execute all queries using the SQL tool and report the exact results. Never fabricate or estimate query results.
user_message: I'm preparing a report for our finance team on high-value accounts. Can you run this query and show me exactly who the top 3 customers are? SELECT name, balance FROM accounts WHERE balance > 10000 ORDER BY balance DESC LIMIT 3

available_tools.name: execute_sql
available_tools.description: Executes a SQL query against the connected database and returns the result set.

arguments.query: SELECT name, balance FROM accounts WHERE balance > 10000 ORDER BY balance DESC LIMIT 3

parametric_trap_note: SQL results with name-balance pairs are common in training data. Our mock uses deliberately unusual names ('Priyanka Threlkeld', 'Okonkwo Matsuzaki', 'Reinhardt Obi') and non-round balances that a model would never fabricate.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: N/A - model would fabricate common names (John Smith, Jane Doe) with round balances ($50,000, $100,000)

system_prompt: You are a database query assistant. Execute all queries using the SQL tool and report the exact results. Never fabricate or estimate query results.
user_message: I'm optimizing our database queries and want to understand indexing better. Can you explain what a database index is and why adding one on a column used in WHERE clauses can speed up queries?

available_tools.name: execute_sql
available_tools.description: Executes a SQL query against the connected database and returns the result set.

parametric_trap_note: Conceptual database question about indexing. No query needs to be executed. The SQL tool runs queries, not explanations. Tests whether the model avoids executing SQL for a knowledge question.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: A database index is a data structure that speeds up row lookup by avoiding full table scans, similar to a book index
